senses.definition: to cause something to stop or finish, often in a permanent way

examples.x: The government put an end to the war by signing a peace treaty.
I am putting an end to this argument.
The fire department put an end to the fire by extinguishing it.
The government decided to put an end to the illegal wildlife trade in the region.

senses.definition: to attend a particular event, gathering, or location, often for a short period of time

examples.x: He decided to put in an appearance at the charity event to show his support.
Even though he was feeling unwell, she made an effort to put in an appearance at the family reunion.
The CEO often puts in an appearance at company meetings to address the employees.
The mayor will put in an appearance at the ribbon-cutting ceremony for the new community center.
Despite her busy schedule, she managed to put in an appearance at the awards ceremony to receive her recognition.

word_list.pronunciation: pňĆ ät ňĆ…õstňĆiňźňąe…™t É ňĆ…™nt ä p…Ļňą√¶kt…™s

senses.definition: to apply a concept or idea in a real-life situation to test its effectiveness or gain experience in using it

examples.x: It's not enough to understand the theory; you need to put it into practice to succeed in this field.
The students were eager to put their knowledge into practice by working on a real engineering project.
She decided to put her new language skills into practice by traveling to a foreign country.
The scientist was eager to put her research findings into practice to make a positive impact on society.

word_list.pronunciation: pňĆ ät ňĆ…õsbňąiňź ňĆ…Ďňźn hňąo äld

senses.definition: to temporarily make someone wait during a phone call before resuming the conversation

examples.x: When the customer called the support hotline, the representative put them on hold to check for information.

word_list.pronunciation: pňĆ ät ňĆ…õstňĆiňźňąe…™t É ňĆ…Ďňźn hňąo äld

senses.definition: to temporarily delay or pause an activity, project, or plan

examples.x: They decided to put the construction project on hold until they secured additional funding.
Due to unexpected circumstances, the company had to put the product launch on hold.
The city put the new park development on hold while they reviewed environmental impact assessments.
We'll put the vacation plans on hold until we can find a suitable time for the trip.

senses.definition: to bring someone to a court of law to determine if they are guilty of a crime

examples.x: It is the responsibility of the legal system to put individuals on trial and ensure a fair and just process.

word_list.pronunciation: pňĆ ät ňĆ…õstňĆiňźňąe…™t É ňĆ…Ďňźn t…Ļňąa…™…ôl

senses.definition: to test or examine something to see if it works or is effective

examples.x: The company decided to put their new software on trial to identify any potential issues before launching it.
Before implementing the policy, they decided to put it on trial to assess its impact on the workforce.
The school put a new teaching method on trial to determine its impact on student learning.
The scientists put the new drug on trial in a series of clinical studies to assess its safety and efficacy.
The marketing team put the ad campaign on trial by running it in a limited market before a full-scale launch.

senses.definition: to gain body mass or become heavier

examples.x: The doctor advised the patient to monitor their diet to avoid putting on weight.

word_list.pronunciation: pňĆ ät …ź stňą…Ďňźp t ä ňĆ…õstňĆiňźňąe…™t É

senses.definition: to make something stop or prevent it from happening, often in a temporary way

examples.x: The police put a stop to the riot by arresting the rioters.
The referee put a stop to the fight by blowing the whistle.
The government put a stop to the protests by declaring a state of emergency.
She is putting a stop to the violence.
The teacher had to put a stop to the students' disruptive behavior in the classroom.

senses.definition: to adjust the time on a clock by moving it ahead, often by one hour, to match the start of daylight saving time

examples.x: Don't forget to put the clock forward by one hour this weekend to align it with daylight saving time.
I put the clock forward on my bedside table before going to sleep, so I wouldn't be late for work the next morning.
Let's put the clock forward before we go to bed so we're not late tomorrow.
It's time to put the clock forward and spring ahead for daylight saving time.

senses.definition: to adjust the time on a clock by moving it backward, often by one hour, to match the end of daylight saving time

examples.x: I put the clock back on the kitchen wall before going to sleep, so I wouldn't wake up an hour too early the next morning.
Remember to put the clock back one hour tonight for the end of daylight saving time.
I always forget to put the clock back in my car after daylight saving time ends.

senses.definition: to encourage someone to do something foolish or inappropriate

senses.definition: to share one's opinion on a topic that is under discussion

word_list.pronunciation: pňĆ ät …™n wňą Ćnz tňąuňź pňą…õn…™wňĆ…úňźőł

senses.definition: to express one's opinion, even if it is not asked for

examples.x: The customer put in his two pennyworth about the new store policy, even though no one asked for his opinion.
The politician put in her two pennyworth about the debate, even though she was not a member of the panel.
The armchair quarterback put in his two pennyworth about the play call, even though he was sitting at home watching the game on TV.
The know-it-all put in their two pennyworth into every conversation, even when no one asked for it.

word_list.pronunciation: pňĆ ät …Ēňź…Ļ …°…õt ňĆ…õstňĆiňźňąe…™t É ňĆ…™nt ä p…öspňą…õkt…™v

senses.definition: to compare something, particularly one's ideas or problems, with those of someone else's in order to better understand, improve,  or deal with them

examples.x: The documentary on global poverty puts the issue into perspective, shedding light on the harsh realities faced by marginalized communities.
Reading historical accounts of past pandemics helps to put the current health crisis into perspective, highlighting the progress made in healthcare.
Visiting impoverished areas abroad can put our privileged lifestyles into perspective and encourage us to be more compassionate.
Discussing the issue with someone who holds a different opinion can help us get the matter into perspective, fostering a more nuanced understanding.

word_list.pronunciation: pňĆ ät …Ēňź…Ļ …°…õt ňĆ…õstňĆiňźňąe…™t É ňĆa ä…ĺ…ôv p…öspňą…õkt…™v

senses.definition: to make something seem less true or important by showing it in a misleading way

examples.x: Leave it to anxiety to put a situation out of perspective.
It's easy to get caught up in the moment and put minor inconveniences out of perspective, but it's important to stay focused on what truly matters.
